schools.summary: Matthew Henry draws a lesson in mutual dependence: even a great king like David needed little Tyre's workmen and materials, so we should despise none but be obliging to all.
Clarke keeps to the plain sequence — Hiram sends artificers and materials to build David a house — cross-referencing the fuller account in 2 Samuel 5.
Ellicott handles the philology (Hiram/Huram, cedar sea-borne from Lebanon, craftsmen of walls and timber) and reads 'house' as a palace, set among Assyrian and Babylonian palace-building.

Ellicott reads Tyre's favour as a sign of God's approval (cf. Genesis 39:21; Luke 2:52) and grounds David's exaltation as national not personal; Keil and Delitzsch parse the unusual passive 'his kingdom had been lifted up' and the Chronicler's intensive 'on high.'

Matthew Henry: it is a satisfaction to see God's providence in one's settlement — 'Who shall unfix me if God hath fixed me?' — and advancement is for usefulness; we are blessed to be blessings (Genesis 12:2).

themes: the favour of man as sign of God

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch carry the record forward; Ellicott notes the Chronicler abbreviates his source ('concubines and wives... after he had come from Hebron') and that the concubines are dropped not for offence, being named at 1 Chronicles 3:9.
Matthew Henry alone presses the tension: David's infirmity in taking more wives, yet the numerous issue added to his honour and strength, for children are a heritage of the LORD (Psalms 127:3).

Clarke counts the versions — eleven names in Samuel's Hebrew, twenty-four in the LXX, thirteen here with all versions agreeing in number though varying in the names — as ordinary wear of transmission.
Ellicott glosses 'his children' as 'the born,' explains the list was copied as an instance of David's prosperity, restores 'and Nathan,' and traces Joseph's descent from Nathan (Luke 3:23).

Keil and Delitzsch list the closing names and refer to the fuller genealogy (1 Chronicles 3:5ff.); Ellicott explains the whole catalogue is copied here as an instance of David's prosperity, the tally of sons a token of divine favour.
Clarke's caution about varieties in the names falls with special force on this verse, whose Beeliada appears elsewhere under another form (cf. 2 Samuel 5:16).
Matthew Henry supplies the thanksgiving: these sons too are a heritage of the LORD (Psalms 127:3), and it was their honour to have such a father.

themes: the tally of sons as prosperity

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: the attack forbids security in any settlement and bids us expect molestation; Christ's kingdom is insulted by the serpent's seed, especially when it advances.
Clarke marks the hostile purpose in going up 'to seek David,' pointing to the fuller narrative in 2 Samuel 5:17.

Ellicott sharpens the wording: 'anointed' (the root of Messiah), the added 'over all Israel' contrasting David's second election with his first, 'to seek' with hostile intent (1 Samuel 26:2), and the stronghold as Zion or Adullam.

Ellicott reads the verse as a resumption ('Now the Philistines had come'), notes the invaders took an unusual road into the valley of Rephaim (cf. 1 Chronicles 11:15), and catches the Chronicler's gentler verb for their 'spreading.'
Matthew Henry makes David's enquiry the chapter's lesson: acknowledge God in all our ways, fly to him in distress, and ask counsel at his oracles before we venture.
Clarke renders the act with the Targum — 'David consulted the Word of the LORD.'
Ellicott explains the enquiry was made through the high priest by Urim and Thummim (Exodus 28:30; Numbers 27:21; 1 Samuel 30:7) and tidies the Hebrew into a single question.
Clarke gives the Targum's expansion: the LORD broke David's enemies like a potter's vessel full of water, shattered at a stroke.

Ellicott recovers Baal-perazim ('owner of breaches'), cites Isaiah 28:21 as proof of the events' moment, and stresses that David owns God's hand in his own valour, unlike the boastful Assyrian (Isaiah 10:15; cf. 1 Chronicles 17:16).
Matthew Henry draws the doxology: bring every sacrifice of praise to God's altar — 'Not unto us, O LORD... but to thy name give glory.'

themes: not unto us but to thy name

schools.summary: Ellicott harmonizes Chronicles' 'burned with fire' with Samuel's 'carried them off' (2 Samuel 5:21) as two moments of one act, and notes the Chronicler records David's obedience to the law against graven images (Deuteronomy 7:25).
Matthew Henry: the burning teaches a holy indignation against idolatry and all its remains — not a keeping of the enemy's images but a resolute destroying of them.
Ellicott notes the Chronicler slightly abridges the source (dropping 'of Rephaim') and repeats the verb of spreading, so the second invasion mirrors the first on the same ground.

Matthew Henry reads the renewed assault as proof that no settlement is secure: Christ's kingdom is insulted by the serpent's seed, especially when it advances, so one victory is no guarantee against the next.
Matthew Henry marks that David enquired again on the fresh occasion — a model that in each new difficulty we should acknowledge God afresh, not presume on yesterday's guidance.

Ellicott adjusts the abridged second half from the older text ('go round to their rear... over against the baca trees') and reads the 'mulberry' as the balsam baca, named from the root 'to weep' (cf. Psalms 84:6).
Clarke reports the Targum's supernatural reading: the sound is the marching of angels coming to David's aid, an angel sent from God's presence to prosper his way.

Ellicott inclines to a natural sign — the wind in the baca tops like men walking on dead leaves — while granting the parallel of 2 Kings 7:6 and the ancient prophetic import of rustling leaves (Judges 9:37), without denying the miracle.
Matthew Henry passes to the obedience: attend God's motions in providence and in the Spirit, and when he goes before us, gird up and follow.

debates.question: What was the 'sound of marching in the tops of the mulberry trees'?

sides.position: a natural sign — the wind rustling the balsam tops, a token divinely timed for David's attack
a supernatural sound — the marching of angels sent from God to David's aid
holders: the Targum (as reported by Adam Clarke)

schools.summary: Ellicott follows the wording of the rout and weighs the reading 'Gibeon' (LXX, Chronicles, favoured by Isaiah 28:21) against 'Geba' (Samuel's Hebrew, Targum, Syriac, Arabic), and glosses 'Gazer' as the pausal form of Gezer (Joshua 12:12).
Matthew Henry: David's success emboldens us to resist our spiritual enemies in dependence on God — resist the devil and he shall flee as the Philistines fled before David.
Clarke keeps the horizon exact: 'into all lands' means the surrounding, neighbouring nations, no others being intended.

Ellicott judges the verse a concluding reflection of the Chronicler's (cf. 2 Chronicles 26:15), and reads even David's renown and the nations' fear as, in the Chronicler's view, the work of Jehovah (cf. Esther 8:17).
Matthew Henry lifts David's fame into type of the exalted honour of the Son of David, to whom God gave a name above every name (Philippians 2:9).

themes: the fear of God on the nations
all of it the work of Jehovah
type of the Son of David
